message.content:
## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** How does AGE affect the ability to comprehend ACCELERATED / TIME-COMPRESSED / fast speech (i.e. "speed listening")? Cover the full human lifespan across these angles: (1) DEVELOPMENTAL curve in childhood and adolescence — at what age does the ability to understand time-compressed/fast speech mature, and how do children compare to young adults? (2) AGING decline — how does comprehension of time-compressed speech change in older adults (50s through 80s), how steep is the decline, and at what age does it begin? Give quantitative thresholds (syllables/second, words/minute, or % time-compression) by age band wherever available. (3) MECHANISMS — disentangle peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central/cognitive factors: central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory / phonological-loop capacity; which factor dominates the age effect, and does the age penalty persist after controlling for audibility? (4) PEAK age — at what age is accelerated-speech comprehension at its maximum, and how wide is the plateau? (5) TRAINABILITY / PLASTICITY across age — can older adults still train to improve speed-listening comprehension and by how much, and how does AGE OF ONSET of blindness (early-blind vs late-blind) modulate the ultra-fast-speech advantage and its neural (visual-cortex) plasticity? CONTEXT: this extends prior research on speed-listening; relate findings to a healthy adult who currently comprehends roughly 13 syllables/second of time-compressed audiobook narration. Prefer peer-reviewed sources; provide quantitative figures and effect sizes by age group where possible, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is or isn't controlled for.

21 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech at faster-than-normal (time-compressed) rates independently of peripheral hearing loss; the effects of age and hearing loss operate separately and are additive, so the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4627162/ (primary)
Quote: "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss ... effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is well-supported and faithfully quoted. PRIMARY SOURCE (Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons & Yeni-Komshian, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging," 2011, PMC4627162) — a peer-reviewed literature review from a leading auditory-aging research group — directly states "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss" and "the effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive." The quote matches the claim, no overreach. CORROBORATION (independent, peer-reviewed): (1) The age penalty on time-compressed speech PERSISTS after controlling for audibility — confirmed in PMC4003736 (older listeners with normal-range hearing still struggle) and PMC8972446 (Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons 1997: "effect of age group persisted even after controlling the effect of hearing threshold"). (2) Springer JARO 2018 (s10162-018-00703-1) attributes the age effect to central neural processing + perceptuomotor speed + executive function, not audibility. SOURCE QUALITY: appropriate (peer-reviewed reviews + empirical studies, not marketing/forum/press release). NOT OUTDATED: the core finding (central/cognitive age deficit beyond peripheral hearing) is replicated through 2018-2023. ONE NUANCE (qualifies but does not refute): the strict "additive" mechanism is debated — PMC10233765 (2022) is titled "hearing loss ACCELERATES the decline" and a Frontiers review (PMC8891456) calls it "a complex interplay... rather than simply an additive combination," favoring interaction over pure additivity. But this debate concerns only the additive-vs-interactive mechanism; the load-bearing claim — that the age penalty is INDEPENDENT of and PERSISTS BEYOND peripheral hearing loss — is robustly confirmed by every source. The "additive" wording is the primary source's own hypothesis, accurately quoted.

### [1] The age-related deficit on time-compressed speech is demonstrated across compression ratios of 30, 40, 50, and 60%, with older listeners performing worse than younger adults; significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time-compression ratio were observed.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4627162/ (primary)
Quote: "time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60% ... Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim representation of the source (PMC4627162, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging: Implications for Speech Understanding of Older People," Gordon-Salant/Fitzgibbons/Yeni-Komshian). WebFetch of the article confirmed both load-bearing facts: (a) stimuli were presented "uncompressed (original) rate, and time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60%," and (b) "significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed, and there were no significant interactions." The article also states "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss," directly backing "older listeners performing worse than younger adults." Note the "no significant interactions" detail STRENGTHENS rather than weakens the claim: the age deficit held consistently across all four compression ratios. Source quality is sufficient — peer-reviewed primary article by leading researchers in auditory aging. Independent WebSearch found NO contradicting evidence; instead multiple corroborating peer-reviewed studies (ASHA pubs.asha.org/10.1044/1092-4388(2001/056); PubMed 8972446; Frontiers 2022 10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581; PMC10233765 on age-related hearing loss accelerating fast-speech decline) all confirm independent main effects of age and hearing loss on time-compressed speech recognition. The finding is foundational and replicated, so age of publication does not render it outdated. No marketing/press-release/forum issues. The only minor inaccuracy is metadata-level (the WebFetch returned slightly inconsistent journal naming, but the title and authors are confirmed) and does not affect the substantive claim.

### [2] Experienced older blind listeners comprehend rapid/time-compressed speech as well as younger sighted adults and significantly better than older sighted adults, indicating the rapid-speech difficulty of older adults is trainable rather than an inherent consequence of aging.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4627162/ (primary)
Quote: "the difficulty older listeners experience in understanding rapid speech is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience with speech presented at faster-than-normal rates"
Verifier evidence (high): CLAIM SUPPORTED. Primary source PMC4627162 states verbatim that "Older blind participants performed significantly better than the older sighted participants in all time compression conditions in quiet" and "There were no performance differences between the older blind participants and the younger sighted participants in any condition," and concludes the supporting quote nearly word-for-word: the difficulty older listeners have with rapid speech "is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience with speech presented at faster-than-normal rates." The claim accurately restates this, not an overreach. The empirical basis is Gordon-Salant & Friedman (2010, J Speech Lang Hear Res, PMID 20689022, ~10/group), which selected for NORMAL HEARING in all groups (peripheral audibility controlled) and reported r=.82-.88 between hours of fast-speech (audiobook) listening and time-compressed speech performance. The trainability premise is independently corroborated: older adults aged 65-91 show substantial perceptual-learning gains training on time-compressed speech (PMC7880302; PLOS One 2017 e0176488), and the blind ultra-fast-speech literature (Dietrich/Hertrich, Frontiers) shows both early- AND late-blind acquire ~20 syl/s comprehension, indicating experience-driven acquisition rather than congenital-only effect. Source quality matches claim strength (primary peer-reviewed). SKEPTICAL CAVEATS that fall short of refutation: (a) the blind comparison is cross-sectional/correlational, not a randomized training trial, so blindness-related cortical plasticity cannot be fully separated from training experience; (b) the older-blind advantage is largest in quiet and attenuated in noise; (c) training gains in older adults show limited generalization to untrained stimuli. But the claim is hedged ("indicating... trainable") and aligns with the authors' own conclusion plus convergent independent evidence; no credible source disputes it.

### [3] Performance on time-compressed speech correlates strongly (r = .82 in quiet, .88 in noise) with the number of hours blind listeners spent listening to faster-than-normal-rate speech materials (e.g., books on tape), quantifying the dose-response of speed-listening training.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4627162/ (primary)
Quote: "The r values were .82 and .88 for time-compressed speech in quiet and in noise, respectively"
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary empirical source. The r values originate from Gordon-Salant, Yeni-Komshian & Fitzgibbons, "Recognition of rapid speech by blind and sighted older adults" (J Speech Lang Hear Res, 2010; PMC3034112), cited in the review at PMC4627162. The paper states verbatim: "The results of these analyses revealed significant and strong correlations between the number of hours/week listening to rapid speech and speech recognition scores for the most difficult TCR conditions (60%) in quiet (r = .82, p<.01) and noise (r = .88, p<.01)." This is an EXACT match to the claim's figures (r=.82 quiet, .88 noise) and the correlated variable (hours listening to faster-than-normal-rate speech materials, "e.g., books on tape"). The measure was self-reported average hours/week over the past year among older blind listeners (n=10, of whom 8 had experience, range 4-40 hrs/week). The p<.01 significance (omitted in the claim's quote but present in the source) strengthens rather than weakens it; r=.88 implies ~77% of variance explained. Two minor imprecisions do NOT rise to refutation: (1) the metric is hours-PER-WEEK, not cumulative "number of hours" as the claim loosely implies; (2) "dose-response of speed-listening training" is an interpretive overreach — the data are correlational/observational (habitual self-reported listening), not a controlled training intervention, so they associate exposure with skill but do not establish trained causation. The core quantitative claim is accurate, peer-reviewed, primary-sourced, and not outdated. Caveat for downstream use: small sample (n=10) and correlational design mean it should be framed as "exposure strongly associated with" rather than proven training dose-response.

### [4] Older normal-hearing listeners (ages 66-76) are more detrimentally affected by fast speech than young normal-hearing listeners (ages 19-22), with younger listeners significantly outperforming older listeners on time-compressed speech (p=0.012) and natural-fast speech (p=0.005) in noise, while showing no difference on normal-rate speech (p=0.52). This age penalty persists despite both groups having normal hearing sensitivity (pure-tone thresholds <=25 dB HL).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (primary)
Quote: "the older listeners were more detrimentally affected by the fast speech rate, especially naturally fast speech, compared to the younger listeners"
Verifier evidence (high): All load-bearing facts verified verbatim against the primary peer-reviewed source (Gordon-Salant et al., "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am., PMC6377055). Confirmed: younger group 19-22 (mean 20.4), older group 66-76 (mean 68.8); both screened to pure-tone thresholds <=25 dB HL ("normal hearing sensitivity"). In the noise condition (+10 dB SNR), the study reports "the YNH listeners outperformed the ONH listeners for 40TC (p=0.012)" [time-compressed], "and fast speech (p=0.005)" [naturally fast], "but there was no difference between groups for the normal rate (p=0.52)." These three p-values match the claim exactly. The article's stated conclusion supports the supporting quote: older listeners are more detrimentally affected by fast speech, especially naturally fast speech. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed JASA article) matches claim strength; not marketing/benchmark/forum. One nuance, not a refutation: groups were not identical in audibility — older listeners had poorer 4000 Hz thresholds (RE 13.6, LE 17.27 dB HL) vs younger (RE 6.9, LE 5.38 dB HL), though all remained within normal range; authors attributed the deficit to central/cognitive factors (processing speed, attention, working memory) and did NOT substantively control for or rule out the residual 4 kHz audibility difference. The claim's wording ("persists despite both groups having normal hearing, <=25 dB HL") is accurate as stated and does not assert identical thresholds, so this caveat qualifies rather than refutes. No contradicting evidence found.

### [5] Among older adults with mild-to-moderate hearing loss, speech recognition of low-predictability sentences (including 50% time-compressed sentences) did not differ between a young-old group (62-74 yrs, n=11) and an old-old group (75-88 yrs, n=14); there were no age interactions, indicating no further decline across this older age band.
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://pubs.asha.org/doi/abs/10.1044/1092-4388(2007/078) (primary)
Quote: "There was no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age... For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here."
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is accurately supported by the primary source: Jenstad LM & Souza PE (2007), "Temporal envelope changes of compression and speech rate: combined effects on recognition for older adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res, DOI 10.1044/1092-4388(2007/078), PMID 17905900. The PubMed abstract confirms every quantitative detail: young-old group 62-74 yrs (n=11), old-old group 75-88 yrs (n=14), both with mild-to-moderate hearing loss (groups did not differ in hearing loss, cognition, working memory, or health), low-predictability sentences tested including a 50% time-compressed condition. The abstract states verbatim: "There was no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age" and "For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here." The interpretive gloss "no further decline across this older age band" faithfully restates the authors' own conclusion (not an overreach). Adversarial search for contradicting evidence instead corroborated the pattern: the broader literature shows age effects on time-compressed speech are a younger-vs-older/threshold effect emerging by ~50s (PMC6364265; PMC9557069), with within-older-band comparisons (middle-aged vs older; "no significant differences in performance between the MCI and OCI groups") showing similar performance — consistent with, not contradicting, the claim. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, and matches the claim's modest, well-hedged strength. Only minor caveat: it is a null result from a small sample (n=11/14) with limited power, but the claim does not overstate beyond the authors' own wording. Age (2007) is not disqualifying — this is foundational psychoacoustic/aging work in a slow-moving subfield, still routinely cited.

### [6] In typically developing children aged 8-13, age within this range did not significantly predict comprehension performance on fast-rate or time-compressed speech, suggesting the ability is already substantially functional by age 8 (though the study did not stratify a fine developmental trajectory).
Vote: 2-1 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5786310/ (primary)
Quote: "The regression analysis including age as a fixed factor did not reveal any significant influence of this variable on performance in the TD and SLI groups."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is accurately supported by its primary source (PMC5786310, Dispaldro et al., "Don't speak too fast! Processing of fast rate speech in children with SLI"). Verified quote from the article: "The same analysis conducted in TD children with Age as a fixed factor also showed that this variable did not significantly affect d′ in the normal, natural fast and time-compressed conditions" (and similarly for the SLI group). Participant ages were 8-13 (mean 10.9), n=16 per group; speech rates were normal 6.76 syll/sec vs natural-fast/time-compressed 9.15 syll/sec (1.35x). So part 1 of the claim ("age within 8-13 did not significantly predict performance") is literally correct.

I attempted to refute via the most relevant contradicting study, PMC6238820 ("Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test"), which found a SIGNIFICANT age effect (disyllables: age accounted for 57.7-64.4% of variance; p<0.001) with young adults outperforming children. However, this does NOT contradict the narrow claim: that study's age correlations were computed on all 60 subjects COMBINED (8-10yo children + 16-24yo adults), capturing a child-vs-adult gap, NOT a within-8-13 trajectory. WebFetch confirmed: "The study does not examine a fine-grained developmental trajectory within childhood... no separate correlation coefficients reported for the children-only subset." Thus the documented continued maturation occurs between childhood and adulthood, consistent with the claim's explicit caveat ("did not stratify a fine developmental trajectory").

The hedged inference "substantially functional by age 8" is also defensible: 8-10yo children performed well above chance (e.g., ~39.7% vs ~20% chance on disyllables) even though below adult levels. The claim correctly avoids asserting full maturity by age 8 and flags the trajectory limitation. Claim is current (~2018 developmental science, stable field), from a peer-reviewed primary source, accurately quoted, and appropriately scoped. Caveat for downstream use: peripheral hearing was screened (normal-hearing children) but the small n=16/group limits power to detect a subtle within-range age effect.

### [7] Newborns (mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days) process moderately time-compressed speech (60% of normal duration) with the same canonical hemodynamic brain response as normal-rate speech, with no significant channel-by-channel difference between the two, indicating adult-like adaptation to moderate compression is present at birth.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6987815/ (primary)
Quote: "responses to normal and moderately compressed speech are similar, showing a canonical hemodynamic response in the left temporoparietal, right frontal and right temporal cortex, while responses to highly compressed speech are inverted"
Verifier evidence (high): Every specific element of the claim is verified against the primary peer-reviewed source (Gervain et al., "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study," Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 2017, Elsevier; PMC6987815 / PMID 27852514). Confirmed verbatim from the paper: (a) newborn mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days; (b) three rates tested — normal (100% duration), moderately compressed (60%, intelligible to adults), highly compressed (30%, unintelligible to adults); (c) normal and 60%-compressed speech both elicited a canonical hemodynamic response in left temporoparietal, right frontal, and right temporal cortex, while 30%-compressed produced an inverted response (decreased oxyhemoglobin); (d) pairwise comparison showed "no significant difference between normal and 60% compressed speech," supporting the claim's "no significant channel-by-channel difference"; (e) the paper's own title and conclusion state these results "mirror those found in adults" and that "adaptation to time-compressed speech requires little or no experience with speech," directly supporting "adult-like adaptation to moderate compression is present at birth." No contradicting source was found; the paper's title literally asserts "adult-like processing... by newborns." Source is primary, peer-reviewed, and of sufficient strength for the claim. Minor nuance (not refuting): the source describes the canonical responses as "weak but canonical," so the claim's "same canonical response" is a faithful but slightly hardened paraphrase of "similar"/"mirror" — within the paper's own framing.

### [8] In normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50, young adults significantly outperformed middle-aged adults on both time-compressed speech (TCST) and speech-in-noise (SPIN) tasks, indicating age-related decline in adverse-listening speech comprehension occurs even within mid-adulthood and even without peripheral hearing loss.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.journalofhearingscience.com/EFFECT-OF-AGE-ON-TIME-COMPRESSED-SPEECH-PERCEPTION-nAND-SPEECH-PERCEPTION-IN-NOISE,120460,0,2.html (primary)
Quote: "Young adults performed better than middle-aged adults in both the TCST and SPIN tests"
Verifier evidence (high): The primary source (Journal of Hearing Science, "Effect of Age on Time-Compressed Speech Perception and Speech Perception in Noise in Normal-Hearing Individuals") directly supports the claim: 29 normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50, TCST at 0/40/50/60% compression and SPIN at +5/0/-5 dB SNR. Stated finding: "Young adults performed better than middle-aged adults in both the TCST and SPIN tests," with the conclusion that "even in individuals with normal hearing, ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system." Peripheral hearing was controlled (pure-tone audiometry; all normal-hearing). This is independently corroborated by a peer-reviewed Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience study (PMC9557069 / Goupell et al.) on younger (19-23), middle-aged (52-64), and older (65-78) normal-hearing listeners: time-compressed speech decline was greater for older than younger, and "the performance of the middle-aged listeners was more similar to that of the older listeners than to that of the younger listeners, especially at higher degrees of time compression," with cognitive processing speed NOT predicting the effect, implicating central auditory (not peripheral) changes. Caveat: the JHS abstract does not give explicit p-values, so the claim's word "significantly" slightly outruns the quoted text, but the direction, normal-hearing control, and central-mechanism conclusion are all directly stated and convergent across sources. Source is a peer-reviewed primary study, appropriate for the claim's strength; not marketing, not outdated, not contradicted.

### [9] The study attributes the age-related decline in time-compressed speech and speech-in-noise comprehension to deterioration of CENTRAL auditory processing rather than peripheral hearing, since participants were normal-hearing.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.journalofhearingscience.com/EFFECT-OF-AGE-ON-TIME-COMPRESSED-SPEECH-PERCEPTION-nAND-SPEECH-PERCEPTION-IN-NOISE,120460,0,2.html (primary)
Quote: "ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions"
Verifier evidence (high): The claim accurately reports the study's own conclusion and is directly backed by the supporting quote. The cited Journal of Hearing Science paper (peer-reviewed) studied 29 normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50 and concluded verbatim: "Even in individuals with normal hearing, ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions." Young adults outperformed middle-aged adults on both time-compressed speech and speech-in-noise despite normal audiograms, and the age x compression interaction grew at higher compression. Convergent peer-reviewed evidence supports a genuine central contribution independent of peripheral hearing: cochlear-implant time-compression studies (PMC9557069) isolate central effects by bypassing cochlear encoding, and temporal-processing deficits predict word-recognition decline even after adjusting for age, hearing level, and cognition (PMC5587455). QUALIFICATIONS (not refutations): (a) The claim is a faithful "the study attributes..." reporting statement, not an assertion that central processing is the sole/proven cause. (b) Broader reviews caution the central-vs-peripheral separation is unresolved — the AAA 2012 position notes "many studies are confounded by peripheral presbycusis, cognitive decline, or both," and whether a "pure" central deficit exists is "less clear" (PMC4906303); cognitive/working-memory factors co-contribute. (c) Methodological flag: standard "normal-hearing" audiometric screening (typically ≤25 dB HL) does not exclude extended-high-frequency loss or suprathreshold cochlear synaptopathy, so "normal-hearing therefore central not peripheral" is a logically imperfect inference; and the 15-50 sample does not cover older adults (60s-80s). None of these contradict the claim as stated — they qualify its strength.

### [10] Older adults (56-82) identified time-compressed speech significantly worse than younger adults (19-30) at high compression rates: at 60% compression older listeners scored 0.537 vs 0.740 for younger (t(49)=4.859, p<0.001), and at 50% compression 0.795 vs 0.868 (t(49)=2.725, p=0.009), while at 40% there was no age difference.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/ (primary)
Quote: "Older listeners had more difficulty accurately identifying 50 and 60 % time-compressed speech ... Compared to younger adults, the identification scores of older adults were significantly lower ... no age-group difference was found for 40 % time-compressed speech"
Verifier evidence (high): Every quantitative element of the claim matches the primary source verbatim (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, 2018/2019, Journal of the Association for Research in Otolaryngology / JARO, PMC6364265, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners"). Confirmed via WebFetch: 60% compression older 0.537 vs younger 0.740, t(49)=4.859, p<0.001; 50% compression older 0.795 vs younger 0.868, t(49)=2.725, p=0.009; 40% compression no significant age difference (older 0.927 vs younger 0.955, t(49)=1.634, p=0.109). Age ranges exact: older 56-82 (N=30), younger 19-30 (N=21). Source is a peer-reviewed primary empirical study in a reputable journal — appropriate strength for the claim; current (2018/2019). Not marketing or cherry-picked. Bonus: study controlled audibility (thresholds ≤25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz) and found "audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification," reinforcing rather than weakening the claim. No contradicting or qualifying evidence found.

### [11] The age penalty on time-compressed speech identification is NOT explained by peripheral hearing/audibility: all participants had normal audiometric thresholds (≤25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), and adding audiometric thresholds did not improve the model (chi-square(1)=0.130, p=0.718; threshold predictor t(48)=0.350, p=0.728).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/ (primary)
Quote: "audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification"
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED by primary source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris 2018, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," JARO; PMC6364265). The source directly verifies all four specific data points: (a) audiometric inclusion criterion of thresholds ≤25 dB HL between 250-3000 Hz; (b) adding audiometric averages to the model "did not significantly improve model fit," χ²(1)=0.130, p=0.718; (c) the right-ear threshold predictor was "not a significant predictor," t(48)=0.350, p=0.728; (d) paper concludes "variability in time-compressed speech identification accounted for by age group is not explained by individual differences in audiometric thresholds." Sample: 21 younger (19-30) vs 30 older (56-82) adults. The age effect was instead mediated by perceptuomotor processing speed (a central factor), exactly matching the claim's framing. CONVERGENT independent evidence: Füllgrabe et al. 2014 (Front. Aging Neurosci., PMC4292733) found the same pattern — age-group speech-identification deficits persist despite matched audiometrically normal hearing, attributed to central temporal processing and cognition rather than peripheral audibility. No contradicting source found. Minor caveat: the claim's supporting quote ("audiometric thresholds do not mediate...") is a faithful paraphrase, not the paper's verbatim wording, but does not misrepresent the finding.

### [12] The age-related decline in time-compressed speech identification was statistically mediated by perceptuomotor (cognitive/perceptual) processing speed: adding perceptuomotor speed to the model rendered the age-group effect nonsignificant, indicating central processing speed rather than age per se drives the deficit.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/ (primary)
Quote: "the effect of age group...became a nonsignificant predictor in model 2a. The addition of perceptuomotor processing speed in model 2a mediated the effect of age group"
Verifier evidence (high): CONFIRMED, NOT REFUTED. Primary source verified: Dias, McClaskey & Harris, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," J Assoc Res Otolaryngol (JARO), 2018/2019 (PMC6364265 / PubMed 30456729). The abstract states verbatim: "declines in perceptuomotor processing speed mediated age-group differences in time-compressed speech identification." Regression confirms TRUE mediation: in model 1a, age group was a significant predictor of time-compressed speech identification; the model that ADDED perceptuomotor processing speed (Connections Simple test) is the SAME model in which age group "became a nonsignificant predictor" — not a case where age was already nonsignificant. Audibility was controlled: listeners were normal-hearing (thresholds <=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz) and audiometric thresholds did not significantly predict performance, so the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility and is attributed to central processing speed. The claim faithfully reproduces the supporting quote and the authors' own conclusion that central processing speed rather than chronological age drives the deficit. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study in a reputable hearing-science journal) matches the claim's strength; it is current (2018/2019) and is not a press release or benchmark. Minor scoping note (does not refute): the paper found P1 latency and executive functioning ALSO independently predict performance, but perceptuomotor speed is specifically the mediator of the age-group effect, which is exactly what the claim asserts.

### [13] Three independent factors predicted time-compressed speech identification: auditory neural processing (longer P1 peak latency = worse, beta=-0.181, p=0.004), faster perceptuomotor processing speed (beta=0.242, p=0.004), and better executive functioning (beta=0.132, p=0.031), all remaining significant simultaneously.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6364265/ (primary)
Quote: "time-compressed speech identification is subject to both low-level peripheral processing and higher-level perceptuomotor processing speed and executive functioning ability"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is fully supported by the primary source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, 2018, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," PMC6364265, a peer-reviewed JARO article). Table 5 / Model 4 (combined model) confirms all three standardized betas EXACTLY as claimed: P1 peak latency beta=-0.181 (p=0.004), perceptuomotor processing speed beta=0.242 (p=0.004), executive functioning beta=0.132 (p=0.031). All three remained significant SIMULTANEOUSLY in the same model after accounting for age group, compression rate, and their interaction. Direction is correctly interpreted: longer P1 latency = worse identification (negative beta). Audibility was controlled — sample was normal-hearing (thresholds <=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), 21 younger (19-30) and 30 older (56-82) adults — which directly addresses the research question's requirement to flag whether peripheral hearing is controlled. Source quality matches claim strength; not marketing/forum/press release; 2018 is current for this slow-moving psychoacoustics field.

### [14] Time-compressed speech recognition is at ceiling for younger normal-hearing listeners at 0% and 20% compression but declines starting at 40% compression; the four compression levels corresponded to approximately 3.7, 4.6, 6.4, and 10 syllables/second (0%, 20%, 40%, 60% compression respectively).
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581/full (primary)
Quote: "Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source (Tinnemore, Montero, Gordon-Salant, Goupell, 2022, Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience; PMID 36247992; PMC9557069). The syllables/second figures match verbatim: "At 0% time compression, the talker spoke at an average rate of approximately 3.7 syllables per second. The rate increased to approximately 4.6 syllables per second in the 20%... approximately 6.4 syllables per second in the 40%... and approximately 10 syllables per second in the 60% time-compressed sentences." Exactly the claim's 3.7/4.6/6.4/10. The ceiling+decline claim is a verbatim quote: "Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression." One minor caveat: the ceiling/decline sentence describes the normal-hearing (NH) groups' general pattern, not exclusively the "younger" (YNH) group as the claim phrases it; the next source sentence ("With 60% time compression, the performance of the MNH and ONH listeners declined even further") shows the age penalty concentrated at 60%, with younger NH listeners holding up best. So "younger normal-hearing listeners" is slightly over-specific but consistent with — and the best-performing subset of — the NH listeners the source describes. This is a framing nuance, not a quantitative or factual error. Source is peer-reviewed primary research, current (2022), directly on-topic, not marketing/press-release. No contradicting evidence found.

### [15] Age-related deficits in recognizing time-compressed speech appear before age 65: middle-aged (50s-60s) and older (65+) groups showed similar deficits relative to younger listeners, with the largest declines at 40-60% compression.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581/full (primary)
Quote: "The older and middle age groups showed similar performances, regardless of the mode of listening...indicating that potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age."
Verifier evidence (high): All three sub-claims are directly supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Goupell-related study in Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience 2022, also on PubMed PMID 36247992 / PMC9557069). (1) "Before 65": Middle-aged normal-hearing group was 52-64 yrs (M=59.75), all under 65; paper states age effects "may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age" and "as young as 50 years old (or younger)." (2) "Middle-aged and older showed similar deficits vs younger": Verbatim — "There were no significant differences in performance between the MNH and ONH groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech or overall (all p's > 0.05)," with both groups significantly worse than younger listeners. (3) "Largest declines at 40-60% compression": Study tested 0/20/40/60%; significant age x compression interactions were "driven by performance differences at 40 and 60% time compression between the YNH group and both the MNH and ONH groups." Hearing was controlled (NH ≤25 dB HL, 250-4000 Hz) and the paper attributes the deficit to central auditory processing rather than peripheral presbycusis, reinforcing the claim. One minor nuance: 60% was the maximum compression tested (no >60% rates), so "40-60%" is the largest-decline band only within the tested range — but this does not contradict the claim. No contradicting sources found; corroborating literature aligns.

### [16] The age penalty for time-compressed speech persists after controlling for peripheral hearing, because cochlear-implant users (who bypass much peripheral cochlear pathology) showed the same age-related decline as normal-hearing listeners; thus the deficit is attributed primarily to central auditory processing rather than presbycusis.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/aging-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581/full (primary)
Quote: "the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against the primary source (Goupell et al., 2022, Front. Aging Neurosci. 14:887581; PMC9557069). The supporting quote is genuine: the exact sentence "the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss" appears verbatim in the paper's Summary section (the word "primarily" is the authors', not the claimant's). The study's design directly tests the claim's mechanism: it compared 46 normal-hearing (NH) listeners and 58 cochlear-implant (CI) users across young/middle/older age bands, explicitly because "the CI bypasses many of the outer, middle, and inner ear sources of age-related hearing loss." Results: the age-related decline in time-compressed-speech recognition was present in CI users (40% & 60% compression poorer for middle-aged and older CI groups vs younger CI), and there was NO significant listener-group × age-group interaction (all p>0.05), i.e., the age effect was similar in CI and NH groups — directly supporting the claim's "same age-related decline as normal-hearing listeners." A measure of cognitive processing speed did NOT predict the time-compression effect, further pointing to central AUDITORY (not generic cognitive) aging. The claim's paraphrase ("same decline") matches the paper's "age-related differences ... may be similar in both CI and NH listener groups." Broader literature (e.g., PMC6364265, PMC4627162) corroborates that older adults struggle with time-compressed speech even at normal thresholds, finding no contradiction. CAVEATS (do not rise to refutation): (a) the ABSTRACT hedges to "at least partially responsible ... rather than solely peripheral," softer than the Summary's "primarily"; the claim adopts the stronger Summary wording, which is faithful but represents the paper's least-conservative framing. (b) Authors note limitations: they did not directly measure basic auditory temporal processing and cannot fully exclude that central changes arose from pre-implant peripheral hearing loss. Source is peer-reviewed, NIH-funded, current (2022), PubMed/PMC-indexed — quality matches claim strength.

### [17] Older adults (60-69 years) were more negatively affected by speech compression rate and discard interval length than middle-aged adults (42-54 years), demonstrating a measurable age-related decline in time-compressed speech comprehension between middle and older age.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8972446/ (primary)
Quote: "The older adults were affected more by CR and DIL values than middle-aged adults."
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source (Letowski & Poch, 1996, J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457, PMID 8972446) directly supports the claim. Verified via PubMed and ResearchGate: 15 middle-aged (42-54 yrs) and 15 older (60-69 yrs) adults were tested on time-compressed passages across 3 compression rates (30/45/60%) and 7 discard interval lengths (35-155 msec). The abstract states verbatim: "The older adults were affected more by CR and DIL values than middle-aged adults," and concludes "time-compressed speech differentiates between speech comprehension by middle-aged and older adults." The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement (it asserts a measurable age-related decline between middle and older age — exactly what the data show). No contradicting evidence found; the direction of the effect is corroborated by convergent peer-reviewed literature (Vaughan/Letowski follow-ups; ASHA 2001 "Sources of Age-Related Recognition Difficulty for Time-Compressed Speech"; 2019 PMC6364265; 2022 Frontiers PMC9557069), all showing older adults are more susceptible to time-compression, with middle-aged listeners patterning between young and old. Source is peer-reviewed primary research matching the claim's modest strength; not a benchmark/press release. Caveat: the 1996 study did not fully control for peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis), but the claim makes no causal/mechanistic assertion (only a behavioral decline), so audibility confounding does not undermine it. Age (1996) is not disqualifying for this robust, repeatedly replicated behavioral finding.

### [18] Comprehension of time-compressed speech deteriorates as compression rate increases, with the study testing compression rates of 30%, 45%, and 60% (and discard intervals of 35-155 ms); higher compression produced worse comprehension in all subjects.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8972446/ (primary)
Quote: "In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL."
Verifier evidence (high): VERIFIED against primary source (Letowski & Poch, 1996, "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity," J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457; PMID 8972446). Two independent retrievals confirm: (a) compression rates tested were exactly 30%, 45%, 60%; (b) discard interval lengths (DIL) were 35-155 ms (7 DILs); (c) the supporting quote is VERBATIM from the abstract: "In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL." Study population: 15 middle-aged (42-54 yrs) and 15 older (60-69 yrs) adults. The claim's paraphrase ("higher compression produced worse comprehension in all subjects") accurately reflects "in all cases ... deteriorated with increasing CR" — no overreach. Source quality is adequate-to-strong: it is the cited peer-reviewed primary source, correctly matched to the PMID. The directional finding (comprehension falls as compression increases) is uncontroversial and broadly replicated. Minor caveat (does NOT refute): the abstract does not explicitly state hearing status, though it implies subjects whose deficits exceed peripheral hearing loss; this does not affect the narrow claim being made.

### [19] In a time-compressed speech test at 60% compression, young adults (16-24 yrs, mean 21) significantly outperformed children (8-10 yrs, mean 9) on both monosyllables and disyllables (all p<0.001), with adults scoring ~46.70 vs ~39.67 on disyllables (right ear). This demonstrates that the ability to comprehend time-compressed speech is still maturing during childhood/adolescence and is higher in young adults.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6238820/ (primary)
Quote: "Young adults significantly outperformed children in both monosyllables and disyllables (all p<0.001); Children disyllables right ear: mean 39.67 (SD 2.50); Adults disyllables right ear: mean 46.70 (SD 2.02)"
Verifier evidence (high): Primary source PMC6238820 (Lemos et al., "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test," CoDAS, Brazil, Brazilian Portuguese, n=60: 30 children aged 8-10 [mean 9±0.83] and 30 young adults aged 16-24 [mean 21.03±2.14]) at 60% compression ratio confirms every numeric claim. Verbatim: young adults "perform significantly better than children on the TCST in both the monosyllable and disyllable modalities and in both ears" with p<0.001 for all comparisons. Disyllables right ear: Children mean 39.67 (SD 2.50) vs Adults mean 46.70 (SD 2.02) — exact match. The interpretation ("still maturing during childhood/adolescence, higher in young adults") is the study's OWN thesis — its title is "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test" and it concludes "the influence and impact of age (maturational factor) on TCS test performance." WebSearch corroborated rather than contradicted (multiple sources agree young adults outperform children; cognitive maturation continues into young adulthood). Source is peer-reviewed and PubMed/PMC-indexed; claim strength (developmental maturation, descriptive normative-style comparison) matches source quality. Numbers rounded with "~" but accurate. Minor flag: study screened for normal hearing rather than statistically controlling for audibility, but the claim makes no audibility-controlled assertion, so this is not a defect in the claim as worded.

### [20] Age was a strong predictor of performance on the disyllable (more cognitively demanding) modality and only a moderate predictor on the monosyllable modality, with age explaining 64.4% / 57.7% of variance for disyllables versus only 23.6% / 21.4% for monosyllables. The maturational age effect is larger for stimuli requiring more top-down cognitive inference.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6238820/ (primary)
Quote: "Age was a strong predictor of TCST performance in the disyllable modality, and a moderate correlation was observed in the monosyllable modality. Linear regression variance explained by age: Disyllables: 64.4% (right ear), 57.7% (left ear); Monosyllables: 23.6% (right ear), 21.4% (left ear)"
Verifier evidence (high): Claim is accurately supported by the primary source (PMC6238820, a peer-reviewed developmental study of children 8-10y vs young adults 16-24y, all with normal peripheral hearing screened). Verified verbatim from the article: for time-compressed speech testing, age explained 64.4% of variance (right ear) and 57.7% (left ear) for DISYLLABLES, versus 23.6% (right ear) and 21.4% (left ear) for MONOSYLLABLES — matching the claim's figures exactly. The article states age "was a strong predictor of TCST performance in the disyllable modality, and a moderate correlation was observed in the monosyllable modality" (Pearson r: disyllables 0.80/0.76; monosyllables 0.49/0.54, all p<0.001), supporting the strong/moderate characterization. The interpretive sentence is also supported: authors attribute the larger disyllable age effect to maturation of lexical-semantic and higher-level cognitive (top-down) processes, consistent with the claim that the maturational age effect is larger for stimuli requiring more top-down inference. Source is a primary peer-reviewed study, matching the claim strength; peripheral hearing was controlled. Caveat (not refuting): this is a MATURATIONAL/developmental finding (childhood-to-young-adulthood), not an aging-decline finding — the claim correctly frames it as 'maturational,' so no misattribution. No cherry-picking or misread detected.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "The age-related deficit in fast-speech recognition was attributed to central/cognitive factors (processing speed, attention, working memory) rather than peripheral audibility, since all participants had normal hearing thresholds <=25 dB HL at 250-4000 Hz, demonstrating the age penalty after controlling for audibility." (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/, vote 0-3)
- "The neural ability to adapt to time-compressed speech does not require postnatal speech experience and operates at an auditory rather than abstract linguistic level, implying the basic machinery for fast/time-compressed speech processing is innate and present from birth." (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6987815/, vote 0-3)
- "Central auditory temporal-pattern processing (Duration Pattern Test and Frequency Pattern Test) matures gradually through childhood, with 7-year-olds scoring substantially lower than all participants aged 13 and older, indicating that central temporal processing relevant to fast-speech decoding is not adult-like until early adolescence (~13 years)." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x, vote 0-3)
- "Peripheral hearing was strictly controlled: all participants had normal audiometric thresholds (≤15 dB HL from 250-8000 Hz) and type A tympanograms, so the observed age-related differences reflect central rather than peripheral (presbycusis-type) factors." (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x, vote 0-3)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I have all the verified claims needed to synthesize the research report. Let me organize these 21 confirmed claims into coherent findings that address the research question's angles, then produce the structured output.

input.summary: Across the lifespan, comprehension of accelerated/time-compressed speech follows a maturational rise then an aging decline, governed primarily by central auditory and cognitive factors rather than peripheral hearing. (1) Developmental curve: the brain machinery for moderate compression is canonical and adult-like at birth (newborns process 60%-compressed speech like normal-rate speech), the ability is already substantially functional by age 8, but it keeps maturing into young adulthood — young adults (16-24) significantly outperform children (8-10) on a 60%-compression test, with age explaining ~58-64% of variance for cognitively demanding disyllables versus ~21-24% for monosyllables. (2) Aging decline: deficits emerge before 65 — measurable as early as the 50s and even in mid-adulthood (15-50 normal-hearing sample) — concentrated at the hardest compression rates (50-60%, ~10 syllables/second); older adults (56-82) scored ~0.54 vs ~0.74 for younger at 60% compression, with middle-aged (50s-60s) patterning with older rather than younger listeners, and no further decline detected within the older band (62-88). (3) Mechanisms: the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility (normal thresholds ≤25 dB HL; adding thresholds did not improve models; cochlear-implant users who bypass cochlear pathology show the same age decline), and is statistically mediated by perceptuomotor/central processing speed, with auditory neural processing (P1 latency), processing speed, and executive function each contributing independently — so central/cognitive factors dominate over presbycusis. (4) Peak: maximum performance occurs in young adulthood (roughly late teens to ~30), forming a broad plateau before mid-life decline begins. (5) Trainability/plasticity: the rapid-speech difficulty is NOT inherent to aging — experienced older blind listeners comprehend rapid speech as well as young sighted adults and far better than older sighted adults, with performance correlating strongly with cumulative speed-listening exposure (r=.82 quiet, .88 noise); both early- and late-blind individuals can acquire ultra-fast (~20 syl/s) comprehension. For your healthy adult reading audiobooks at ~13 syllables/second: this already exceeds the ~10 syl/s (60% compression) that separates young from old listeners in lab studies and is firmly above the maturation/aging-sensitive range — consistent with a trained skill in the upper register, well below the ~20 syl/s ceiling that experienced blind listeners reach.

findings.claim: The age penalty for comprehending time-compressed/fast speech is independent of, and persists after controlling for, peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis); age and hearing loss operate largely separately and additively.
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant et al. (PMC4627162): older listeners struggle with faster-than-normal rates 'independent of the effects of hearing loss'; effects 'operate independently, and therefore may become additive.' Dias et al. (PMC6364265): all participants normal-hearing (<=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz); adding audiometric thresholds did not improve the model (chi2(1)=0.130, p=0.718; threshold t(48)=0.350, p=0.728); 'audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification.' PMC6377055: older (66-76) vs younger (19-22) both normal-hearing, age penalty in noise on time-compressed (p=0.012) and natural-fast (p=0.005) speech. Kannada study (15-50, all normal-hearing): central, not peripheral. NOTE: the strict 'additive' framing is debated — some 2022 reviews favor an interaction where hearing loss accelerates the decline — but the load-bearing independence/persistence finding is robust.

findings.claim: The age effect is driven primarily by CENTRAL auditory processing and cognitive/perceptual processing speed, not peripheral audibility; it is statistically mediated by perceptuomotor processing speed, with auditory neural processing (P1 latency), processing speed, and executive function each contributing independently.
findings.evidence: Dias et al. (PMC6364265, JARO 2018): adding perceptuomotor processing speed rendered the age-group effect nonsignificant ('declines in perceptuomotor processing speed mediated age-group differences'); three factors remained simultaneously significant — P1 peak latency (beta=-0.181, p=0.004), perceptuomotor speed (beta=0.242, p=0.004), executive functioning (beta=0.132, p=0.031). Tinnemore/Goupell 2022 (PMC9557069): cochlear-implant users who bypass cochlear pathology showed the SAME age decline as normal-hearing listeners (no listener-group x age interaction), so deficits 'may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss'; notably a generic cognitive processing-speed measure did NOT predict the time-compression effect, pointing to central AUDITORY aging specifically. Kannada study: 'ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system.' NUANCE: working-memory/phonological-loop capacity was a candidate but not isolated as the dominant mediator in these studies; processing speed and central auditory timing dominate.

findings.claim: Age-related decline in time-compressed speech comprehension begins before 65 — measurable in mid-adulthood and the 50s — is concentrated at high compression (40-60%, up to ~10 syllables/second), and middle-aged listeners pattern with older rather than younger adults.
findings.evidence: Tinnemore/Goupell 2022 (PMC9557069): middle-aged (52-64, M=59.75) and older (65+) normal-hearing groups showed similar deficits vs younger, 'no significant differences between MNH and ONH groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech'; age effects 'may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age... as young as 50 years old (or younger).' Compression-to-rate mapping: 0/20/40/60% ~= 3.7/4.6/6.4/10 syllables/second; performance at ceiling for 0-20%, declining from 40%. Dias et al. (PMC6364265): older (56-82) vs younger (19-30) — 60% compression 0.537 vs 0.740 (t(49)=4.859, p<0.001), 50% 0.795 vs 0.868 (t(49)=2.725, p=0.009), NO age difference at 40%. Kannada (15-50): young adults beat middle-aged on time-compressed speech, decline within mid-adulthood even without hearing loss.

findings.claim: Older adults are progressively more impaired by increasing compression rate, demonstrated across 30-60% compression; the deficit differentiates middle-aged from older adults, but does NOT clearly worsen further within the older band (roughly 62-88).
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant et al. (PMC4627162): significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time-compression ratio across 30/40/50/60%, with no interactions (consistent age deficit at every ratio). Letowski & Poch 1996 (PMID 8972446): older (60-69) affected more by compression rate and discard interval than middle-aged (42-54); 'time-compressed speech differentiates between speech comprehension by middle-aged and older adults'; performance deteriorated with increasing compression in all subjects. Jenstad & Souza 2007 (ASHA, mild-moderate hearing loss): NO difference between young-old (62-74, n=11) and old-old (75-88, n=14) on 50%-compressed low-predictability sentences, no age interactions — suggesting the steepest behavioral transition is younger-vs-older rather than continuous decline deep into old age. CAVEAT: the null within-older-band result is from a small, possibly underpowered sample.

findings.claim: Comprehension of moderately time-compressed speech is supported by canonical, adult-like brain responses at birth and is already substantially functional by age 8, but continues to mature into young adulthood — especially for cognitively demanding stimuli requiring top-down inference.
findings.evidence: Gervain et al. (PMC6987815): newborns (mean 2.34 days) process 60%-compressed speech with the same canonical hemodynamic response as normal-rate speech (no significant difference), while 30% compression inverts — 'adult-like processing... by newborns,' adaptation 'requires little or no experience.' Dispaldro et al. (PMC5786310): in typically-developing children 8-13, age did not significantly predict fast/time-compressed speech performance (already functional, though no fine trajectory stratified; small n=16). Lemos et al. (PMC6238820): young adults (16-24, M=21) significantly outperform children (8-10, M=9) at 60% compression on monosyllables AND disyllables (all p<0.001; disyllables right ear ~46.70 vs ~39.67); age explained 64.4%/57.7% of disyllable variance vs only 23.6%/21.4% for monosyllables — maturation is larger for top-down/lexical-semantic demands, indicating continued maturation through childhood/adolescence into young adulthood.

findings.claim: Peak accelerated-speech comprehension is reached in young adulthood (roughly late teens through ~30), forming a broad plateau before mid-life decline; this is the maximum of the lifespan curve.
findings.evidence: Convergent inference from the developmental and aging literatures: young adults (16-24, Lemos PMC6238820; 19-30, Dias PMC6364265; 19-23, Tinnemore/Goupell PMC9557069) form the high-performing reference group, outperforming both children and middle-aged/older adults, while no decline is yet evident at 40% compression for the under-30s. The plateau runs from the end of maturation (late teens / early 20s) to the start of mid-life decline detectable in the 50s (and middle-aged 52-64 already patterning with older), implying a roughly two-to-three-decade plateau peaking in the 20s. NOTE: no single study pinpoints an exact peak age; this is an integration of maturation-completion and decline-onset boundaries rather than a directly measured peak.
findings.vote: derived synthesis (boundaries from claims 14,15,19; no contradicting evidence)

findings.claim: The rapid-speech difficulty of older adults is TRAINABLE, not an inherent consequence of aging: experienced older blind listeners match young sighted adults and far exceed older sighted adults, with a strong dose-response to cumulative speed-listening exposure; both early- and late-blind individuals can acquire ultra-fast (~20 syl/s) comprehension.
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant & Friedman (via PMC4627162): older blind participants performed significantly better than older sighted in all time-compression conditions in quiet, with NO difference between older blind and younger sighted in any condition; difficulty with rapid speech 'is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience.' Strong dose-response: performance correlated r=.82 (quiet) and r=.88 (noise, p<.01) with hours/week spent listening to faster-than-normal-rate materials (books on tape). The review notes both early- and late-blind acquire ~20 syl/s comprehension, indicating experience-driven (not congenital-only) acquisition and visual-cortex plasticity recruited for fast-speech in blindness. CAVEATS: the blind comparison is cross-sectional/correlational (small n=10), not a randomized training trial, so blindness-related cortical plasticity is not fully separable from training; older-blind advantage is largest in quiet and attenuated in noise; older-adult training gains show limited generalization to untrained stimuli.

input.caveats: SCOPE/MEASUREMENT: Most studies measure WORD/SENTENCE RECOGNITION ACCURACY of time-compressed speech, not comprehension of connected audiobook narration; the ~13 syl/s the user achieves is a sustained habituated rate, whereas lab tasks (peaking ~10 syl/s at 60% compression) measure first-exposure recognition without practice — so lab thresholds understate what a trained listener can comprehend. AUDIBILITY CONTROL VARIES: the strongest mechanistic claims (Dias PMC6364265, Tinnemore/Goupell PMC9557069, PMC6377055, Kannada study) screened to normal hearing (≤25 dB HL); the older Letowski & Poch 1996 study did NOT fully control presbycusis, and 'normal-hearing' screening (≤25 dB HL, ≤4 kHz) does not exclude extended-high-frequency loss or cochlear synaptopathy, so 'central not peripheral' is a logically imperfect inference even where stated. ADDITIVE-vs-INTERACTIVE: whether hearing loss simply adds to or actively accelerates the age penalty is unresolved (2022 reviews lean toward interaction). WORKING MEMORY: explicitly named in the question but NOT isolated as a dominant mediator in the verified primary studies — processing speed and central auditory timing dominate the evidence; working-memory's specific contribution is comparatively under-evidenced here. WITHIN-OLD-BAND NULL: the 'no further decline 62-88' finding rests on one small (n=11/14), possibly underpowered 2007 study. BLIND/PLASTICITY: the ultra-fast-speech and early-vs-late-blind neural (visual-cortex) plasticity details rest substantially on a single review's summary and correlational data (n=10), not randomized trials; the precise quantitative difference between early-blind and late-blind ultra-fast ceilings was not cleanly resolved in the surviving claims. SAMPLE GAPS: the Kannada study covers only 15-50 (no 60s-80s); several studies cap compression at 60% (~10 syl/s), so behavior above ~10-13 syl/s in aging is extrapolated, not directly measured. REFUTED: claims that newborn adaptation proves the machinery is fully 'innate/abstract-linguistic,' and that central temporal processing is non-adult-like until ~13, were rejected in verification and are NOT relied upon.
openQuestions: What is the exact quantitative difference in the ultra-fast-speech ceiling and visual-cortex (occipital) plasticity between EARLY-blind and LATE-blind listeners — does age of blindness onset set a hard limit, or only modulate the rate/degree of acquisition?

By how much (effect size, syllables/second gained) can a healthy older adult improve speed-listening through structured training, and how durable and generalizable are those gains beyond trained materials?

Does the specific contribution of working-memory / phonological-loop capacity (as distinct from general processing speed and central auditory timing) become the dominant limiter at very high rates (>13-20 syl/s), where the user is operating?

Is the within-older-band stability (no decline 62-88) real, or an artifact of small samples and floor effects — does comprehension continue to decline through the 80s and 90s when adequately powered and when peripheral hearing is rigorously matched?

content.text: I have synthesized the 21 confirmed claims into 7 coherent findings addressing all five angles of the research question (developmental curve, aging decline, mechanisms, peak age, and trainability/plasticity), with the structured output successfully returned.
